([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Πήγασος, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ancient_Greek "Romanization of Ancient Greek"): *Pḗgasos*; [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Pegasus, Pegasos*) is a [winged horse](/wiki/Winged_horse "Winged horse") in [Greek mythology](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology"), usually depicted as a white stallion. He was sired by [Poseidon](/wiki/Poseidon "Poseidon"), in his role as horse-god, and foaled by the [Gorgon](/wiki/Gorgon "Gorgon") [Medusa](/wiki/Medusa "Medusa"). Pegasus was the brother of [Chrysaor](/wiki/Chrysaor "Chrysaor"), both born from Medusa's blood when their mother was decapitated by [Perseus](/wiki/Perseus "Perseus"). Greco-Roman poets wrote about his ascent to heaven after his birth and his obeisance to [Zeus](/wiki/Zeus "Zeus"), who instructed him to bring lightning and thunder from [Olympus](/wiki/Mount_Olympus "Mount Olympus").

Pegasus attacking the [Chimera](/wiki/Chimera_(mythology) "Chimera (mythology)") with his hooves, above the hero [Bellerophon](/wiki/Bellerophon "Bellerophon"), on a [Laconian](/wiki/Laconia "Laconia") black-figure [kylix](/wiki/Kylix "Kylix"), attributed to the Boreads Painter and dating to c. 575–550 BC[[1]](#cite_note-1)

**Pegasus** ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Πήγασος, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ancient_Greek "Romanization of Ancient Greek"): *Pḗgasos*; [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Pegasus, Pegasos*) is a [winged horse](/wiki/Winged_horse "Winged horse") in [Greek mythology](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology"), usually depicted as a white stallion. He was sired by [Poseidon](/wiki/Poseidon "Poseidon"), in his role as horse-god, and foaled by the [Gorgon](/wiki/Gorgon "Gorgon") [Medusa](/wiki/Medusa "Medusa"). Pegasus was the brother of [Chrysaor](/wiki/Chrysaor "Chrysaor"), both born from Medusa's blood when their mother was decapitated by [Perseus](/wiki/Perseus "Perseus"). Greco-Roman poets wrote about his ascent to heaven after his birth and his obeisance to [Zeus](/wiki/Zeus "Zeus"), who instructed him to bring lightning and thunder from [Olympus](/wiki/Mount_Olympus "Mount Olympus").

Pegasus is the creator of [Hippocrene](/wiki/Hippocrene "Hippocrene"), the fountain on [Mount Helicon](/wiki/Mount_Helicon "Mount Helicon"). He was captured by the Greek hero [Bellerophon](/wiki/Bellerophon "Bellerophon"), near the fountain [Peirene](/wiki/Pirene_(fountain) "Pirene (fountain)"), with the help of [Athena](/wiki/Athena "Athena") and Poseidon. Pegasus allowed Bellerophon to ride him in order to defeat the monster [Chimera](/wiki/Chimera_(mythology) "Chimera (mythology)"), which led to many more exploits. Bellerophon later fell from Pegasus's back while trying to reach [Mount Olympus](/wiki/Mount_Olympus "Mount Olympus"). Both Pegasus and Bellerophon were said to have died at the hands of Zeus for trying to reach Olympus. Other tales have Zeus bring Pegasus to Olympus to carry his thunderbolts.

Long honored [as a constellation](/wiki/Pegasus_(constellation) "Pegasus (constellation)"), Pegasus is a subject of very rich iconography, especially through [ancient Greek pottery](/wiki/Pottery_of_ancient_Greece "Pottery of ancient Greece") as well as paintings and sculptures of the Renaissance.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Pelike_Bellerophon_Louvre_G535.jpg/250px-Pelike_Bellerophon_Louvre_G535.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pelike_Bellerophon_Louvre_G535.jpg)

Bellerophon mounted on Pegasus fighting the Chimera, side A from an Attic red-figure pelike

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Bellerophon_riding_Pegasus_and_killing_the_Chimera%2C_Roman_mosaic%2C_the_Rolin_Museum_in_Autun%2C_France%2C_2nd_to_3rd_century_AD.jpg/250px-Bellerophon_riding_Pegasus_and_killing_the_Chimera%2C_Roman_mosaic%2C_the_Rolin_Museum_in_Autun%2C_France%2C_2nd_to_3rd_century_AD.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bellerophon_riding_Pegasus_and_killing_the_Chimera,_Roman_mosaic,_the_Rolin_Museum_in_Autun,_France,_2nd_to_3rd_century_AD.jpg)

[Bellerophon](/wiki/Bellerophon "Bellerophon") riding Pegasus and slaying the [Chimera](/wiki/Chimera_(mythology) "Chimera (mythology)"), central medallion of [a Gallo-Roman mosaic](/wiki/Bellerophon_Mosaic "Bellerophon Mosaic") from [Autun](/wiki/Autun "Autun"), [Musée Rolin](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_Rolin "Musée Rolin"), 2nd to 3rd century AD

The poet [Hesiod](/wiki/Hesiod "Hesiod") presents a [folk etymology](/wiki/Folk_etymology "Folk etymology") of the name *Pegasus* as derived from πηγή *pēgē* 'spring, well', referring to "the *pegai* of [Okeanos](/wiki/Oceanus "Oceanus"), where he was born".[[2]](#cite_note-2)

A proposed etymology of the name is [Luwian](/wiki/Luwian_language "Luwian language") *pihassas* 'lightning', and *Pihassassi*, a local Luwian-[Hittite](/wiki/Hittite_language "Hittite language") name in southern [Cilicia](/wiki/Cilicia "Cilicia") of a weather deity associated with thunder and lightning. The proponents of this etymology adduce the role of Pegasus, reported as early as [Hesiod](/wiki/Hesiod "Hesiod"), as the bringer of thunderbolts to Zeus. That interpretation was first suggested in 1952 and remains widely accepted,[[3]](#cite_note-3) but [Robin Lane Fox](/wiki/Robin_Lane_Fox "Robin Lane Fox") (2009) has criticized it as implausible.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Springs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Springs")]

According to early myths, everywhere the winged horse struck his hoof to the earth, an inspiring water spring burst forth. One of these springs was upon the [Muses](/wiki/Muse "Muse")' [Mount Helicon](/wiki/Mount_Helicon "Mount Helicon"), the *[Hippocrene](/wiki/Hippocrene "Hippocrene")* ("horse spring").[[5]](#cite_note-5) [Antoninus Liberalis](/wiki/Antoninus_Liberalis "Antoninus Liberalis") suggested,[[6]](#cite_note-6) that it was opened at the behest of [Poseidon](/wiki/Poseidon "Poseidon") to prevent the [mountain](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain") from swelling with rapture at the song of the Muses. Another spring associated with Pegasus was at [Troezen](/wiki/Troezen "Troezen").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Hesiod relates how Pegasus was peacefully drinking from a spring when the hero [Bellerophon](/wiki/Bellerophon "Bellerophon") captured him.

## Thunderbolts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Thunderbolts")]

[Hesiod](/wiki/Hesiod "Hesiod") wrote that Pegasus carried [thunderbolts](/wiki/Thunderbolt "Thunderbolt") for [Zeus](/wiki/Zeus "Zeus").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Roman iconography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Roman iconography")]

McCartney examines early Roman *aes signatum* (cast bronze currency) that pairs Pegasus with an eagle carrying a thunderbolt. He interprets this imagery in light of literary traditions that describe Pegasus as bearing Zeus’s thunder or drawing the god’s storm chariot, suggesting that Roman iconography viewed Pegasus as linked to divine power and storm symbolism.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Birth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Birth")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/NAMA_Epinetron_Bell%C3%A9rophon.jpg/250px-NAMA_Epinetron_Bell%C3%A9rophon.jpg)](/wiki/File:NAMA_Epinetron_Bell%C3%A9rophon.jpg)

Bellerophon and the Chimera, edge of an Attic red-figure epinetron (thigh-protector used by a woman when weaving)

There are several versions of the birth of the winged stallion and his brother [Chrysaor](/wiki/Chrysaor "Chrysaor") in the far distant place at the edge of Earth, Hesiod's "springs of Oceanus", which encircles the inhabited earth, where [Perseus](/wiki/Perseus "Perseus") found [Medusa](/wiki/Medusa "Medusa"):

One is that they sprang from the blood issuing from Medusa's neck as [Perseus](/wiki/Perseus "Perseus") was beheading her,[[10]](#cite_note-10) similar to the manner in which [Athena](/wiki/Athena "Athena") was born from the head of Zeus after he swallowed her pregnant mother.

In another version, when Perseus beheaded Medusa, the brothers were born of the Earth, when the Gorgon's blood fell upon her. A variation of this story holds that they were formed from the mingling of Medusa's blood, pain, and sea foam, implying that Poseidon had involvement in their making.

The last version bears resemblance to [Hesiod](/wiki/Hesiod "Hesiod")'s account of the birth of [Aphrodite](/wiki/Aphrodite "Aphrodite") from the foam created when the severed genitals of [Uranus](/wiki/Uranus_(mythology) "Uranus (mythology)") were cast into the sea by [Cronus](/wiki/Cronus "Cronus").

## Bellerophon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Bellerophon")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Bellerophon_Khimaira_Louvre_A478.jpg/250px-Bellerophon_Khimaira_Louvre_A478.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bellerophon_Khimaira_Louvre_A478.jpg)

Bellerophon fighting the Chimera on an Athenian black-figure Siana cup found in Camiros (Rhodes)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Bellerophon_killing_Chimaera_%28mosaic_from_Rhodes%29.jpg/250px-Bellerophon_killing_Chimaera_%28mosaic_from_Rhodes%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bellerophon_killing_Chimaera_(mosaic_from_Rhodes).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Bellerophon_mosaic%2C_N%C3%AEmes_-_panel_5_Bellerophon_%28N%C3%AEmes%2C_Mus_Arch%29.jpg/250px-Bellerophon_mosaic%2C_N%C3%AEmes_-_panel_5_Bellerophon_%28N%C3%AEmes%2C_Mus_Arch%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bellerophon_mosaic,_N%C3%AEmes_-_panel_5_Bellerophon_(N%C3%AEmes,_Mus_Arch).jpg)

Left: a [Hellenistic Greek](/wiki/Hellenistic_art "Hellenistic art") [mosaic](/wiki/Mosaic "Mosaic") of Bellerophon slaying the Chimera, 300–270 BC, [Archaeological Museum of Rhodes](/wiki/Archaeological_Museum_of_Rhodes "Archaeological Museum of Rhodes")Right: a [Roman mosaic](/wiki/Roman_mosaic "Roman mosaic") of Bellerophon slaying the Chimera, 2nd to 3rd centuries AD, [Musée de la Romanité](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_de_la_Romanit%C3%A9 "Musée de la Romanité"), [Nîmes](/wiki/N%C3%AEmes "Nîmes")

Pegasus aided the hero [Bellerophon](/wiki/Bellerophon "Bellerophon") in his fight against the [Chimera](/wiki/Chimera_(mythology) "Chimera (mythology)"). There are varying tales about how Bellerophon found Pegasus; the most common[[11]](#cite_note-11) being that the hero was told by [Polyeidos](/wiki/Polyeidos "Polyeidos") to sleep in the [temple of Athena](/wiki/Parthenon "Parthenon"), where the goddess visited him in the night and presented him with a golden bridle. The next morning, still clutching the bridle, Bellerophon found Pegasus drinking at the [Pierian](/wiki/Pierian "Pierian") spring, caught him, and eventually tamed him.

### Ancient interpretations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Ancient interpretations")]

Hubbard discusses multiple ancient accounts of Pegasus’s capture, including versions in Hesiod, Pindar, and Corinthian cult tradition. He highlights Athena’s role in providing the bridle that allowed Pegasus to be tamed and connects this motif to the Corinthian cult of Athena Hippia/Chalinitis. Hubbard also notes Pegasus’s appearance on early Corinthian coinage, where the winged horse served as a civic symbol.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Perseus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Perseus")]

Michaud's *Biographie universelle* relates that when Pegasus was born, he flew to where thunder and lightning are released. Then, according to certain versions of the myth, Athena tamed him and gave him to Perseus, who flew to [Ethiopia](/wiki/Aethiopia "Aethiopia") to help [Andromeda](/wiki/Andromeda_(mythology) "Andromeda (mythology)").[[13]](#cite_note-Michaud-13)

## Olympus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Olympus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Stat%C3%A8re_repr%C3%A9sentant_P%C3%A9gase.jpg/250px-Stat%C3%A8re_repr%C3%A9sentant_P%C3%A9gase.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stat%C3%A8re_repr%C3%A9sentant_P%C3%A9gase.jpg)

Silver coin of [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily"): *obverse*, head of Athena wearing a Corinthian helmet adorned with a griffin; *reverse*, Pegasus flying and beneath him a [triskeles](/wiki/Triskeles "Triskeles")

After Bellerophon fell off Pegasus while trying to reach Olympus, Pegasus and Athena left him and continued to Olympus where he was stabled with other steeds belonging to [Zeus](/wiki/Zeus "Zeus"), and was given the task of carrying Zeus's thunderbolts, along with other members of his entourage, his [attendants](/wiki/Servant "Servant")/[handmaidens](/wiki/Handmaiden "Handmaiden")/[shield bearers](/wiki/Shield_bearer "Shield bearer")/[shieldmaidens](/wiki/Shieldmaiden "Shieldmaiden"), [Astrape and Bronte](/wiki/Astrape_and_Bronte "Astrape and Bronte").

Because of his years of faithful service to Zeus, Pegasus was later honoured with transformation into a [constellation](/wiki/Pegasus_(constellation) "Pegasus (constellation)").[[14]](#cite_note-14) On the day of his *[catasterism](/wiki/Catasterism "Catasterism")*, when Zeus transformed him into a constellation, a single feather fell to Earth near the city of [Tarsus](/wiki/Tarsus_(city) "Tarsus (city)").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Indo-European context

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Indo-European context")]

Lobell and Powell situate Pegasus within the wider Indo-European tradition of divine or supernatural horses. They note that Pegasus’s transformation into a constellation reflects ancient associations between equine imagery and seasonal renewal, with the Pegasus constellation linked to the arrival of spring in several early traditions.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Legacy")]

### In heraldry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: In heraldry")]

The pegasus became a common element in British heraldry, appearing chiefly as a [supporter](/wiki/Supporter "Supporter") or a [crest](/wiki/Crest_(heraldry) "Crest (heraldry)"). Pegasi may also appear upon [escutcheons](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)"), although this is rare. A pegasus rampant is featured on the arms of the [Inner Temple](/wiki/Inner_Temple "Inner Temple"), while those of the Richardson family contain a rare depiction of a pegasus sejant.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### World War II emblem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: World War II emblem")]

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the silhouetted image of Bellerophon the warrior, mounted on the winged Pegasus, was adopted by the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")'s newly raised parachute troops in 1941 as their upper sleeve insignia.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/British_Airborne_Units.svg/250px-British_Airborne_Units.svg.png)](/wiki/File:British_Airborne_Units.svg)

The emblem of the World War II, British Airborne Forces, [Bellerophon](/wiki/Bellerophon "Bellerophon") riding the flying horse Pegasus

The image clearly symbolized a warrior arriving at a battle by air, the same tactics used by [paratroopers](/wiki/Paratrooper "Paratrooper"). The square upper-sleeve insignia comprised Bellerophon/Pegasus in light blue on a maroon background. One source suggests that the insignia was designed by famous English novelist [Daphne du Maurier](/wiki/Daphne_du_Maurier "Daphne du Maurier"), who was wife of the commander of the [1st Airborne Division](/wiki/1st_Airborne_Division_(United_Kingdom) "1st Airborne Division (United Kingdom)") (and later the expanded British Airborne Forces), General [Frederick "Boy" Browning](/wiki/Frederick_Browning "Frederick Browning"). According to the British Army Website, the insignia was designed by the celebrated East Anglian painter Major [Edward Seago](/wiki/Edward_Seago "Edward Seago") in May 1942. The maroon background on the insignia was later used again by the Airborne Forces when they adopted the famous maroon beret in Summer 1942. The beret was the origin of the German nickname for British airborne troops, the [Red Devils](/wiki/Red_Devils_(Parachute_Regiment) "Red Devils (Parachute Regiment)"). Today's [Parachute Regiment](/wiki/Parachute_Regiment_(United_Kingdom) "Parachute Regiment (United Kingdom)") carries on the maroon beret tradition. The selection process for the elite Parachute Regiment is called [Pegasus Company](/wiki/Pegasus_Company "Pegasus Company") (often abbreviated to "P Company").

In 2015 it was announced that the units of [16 Air Assault Brigade](/wiki/16_Air_Assault_Brigade "16 Air Assault Brigade") would once again use the Pegasus insignia after a 15-year hiatus.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

During the [airborne phase](/wiki/Operation_Deadstick "Operation Deadstick") of the [Normandy invasion](/wiki/Invasion_of_Normandy "Invasion of Normandy") on the night of 5–6 June 1944, [British 6th Airborne Division](/wiki/6th_Airborne_Division_(United_Kingdom) "6th Airborne Division (United Kingdom)") captured all its key objectives in advance of the seaborne assault, including the capture and holding at all costs of a vital bridge over the [Caen Canal](/wiki/Canal_de_Caen_%C3%A0_la_Mer "Canal de Caen à la Mer"), near [Ouistreham](/wiki/Ouistreham "Ouistreham"). In memory of their tenacity, the bridge has been known ever since as [Pegasus Bridge](/wiki/Pegasus_Bridge "Pegasus Bridge").

### Tuscany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Tuscany")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Italian_16th_Century_%28Attributed_to_Benvenuto_Cellini%29%2C_Pegasus_on_the_Fountain_Hippocrene_%28reverse%29%2C_1537-1547%2C_NGA_45081.jpg/250px-Italian_16th_Century_%28Attributed_to_Benvenuto_Cellini%29%2C_Pegasus_on_the_Fountain_Hippocrene_%28reverse%29%2C_1537-1547%2C_NGA_45081.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italian_16th_Century_(Attributed_to_Benvenuto_Cellini),_Pegasus_on_the_Fountain_Hippocrene_(reverse),_1537-1547,_NGA_45081.jpg)

Benvenuto Cellini's 1537 coin.

The Pegasus has been a symbol of Tuscany ever since [Benvenuto Cellini](/wiki/Benvenuto_Cellini "Benvenuto Cellini") incorporated it in a coin made in 1537 to honor Cardinal [Pietro Bembo](/wiki/Pietro_Bembo "Pietro Bembo").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The [Tuscan Committee of National Liberation](/wiki/Tuscan_Committee_of_National_Liberation "Tuscan Committee of National Liberation") during the German occupation of Italy also had a Pegasus as its emblem. The winged horse is still featured on the [Tuscan flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Tuscany "Flag of Tuscany") and coat of arms.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Pegasus_Airlines.JPG/250px-Pegasus_Airlines.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pegasus_Airlines.JPG)

Wing of the [Pegasus Airlines](/wiki/Pegasus_Airlines "Pegasus Airlines") plane

### In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pegasus&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

Main article: [Pegasus in popular culture](/wiki/Pegasus_in_popular_culture "Pegasus in popular culture")

The winged horse has provided an instantly recognizable corporate logo or emblem of inspiration. [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador") launched its weather satellite, named *Pegaso* (pronounced [[peˈɣaso]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"), Pegasus in Spanish), on 26 April 2013 but it was damaged by Russian space debris.[[20]](#cite_note-20) [Pegasus Airlines](/wiki/Pegasus_Airlines "Pegasus Airlines") (Turkish: Pegasus Hava Taşımacılığı A.Ş.) is a low-cost airline headquartered in the Kurtköy area of Pendik, [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"). [Mobil](/wiki/Mobil "Mobil") Oil has had a Pegasus as its company logo since its affiliation with [Magnolia Petroleum Company](/wiki/Magnolia_Petroleum_Company "Magnolia Petroleum Company") in the 1930s. [TriStar Pictures](/wiki/TriStar_Pictures "TriStar Pictures") famously uses a winged horse in their logo.
